sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypertension (high blood pressure) is defined as a persistent elevation of blood pressure (BP) in the arteries and is typically asymptomatic (“silent killer”). Hypertension can be primary (essential [90-95% of cases]) where it has no single identifiable cause and may develop due to genetic and lifestyle factors, or secondary which is caused by an underlying medical condition.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, (especially occipital in the mornings)
- Visual disturbances, dizziness
- Chest pain, palpitations
- Shortness of breath
- Peripheral oedema
- Epistaxis (nose bleeds)
- ECG changes (hypertrophy, LBBB, etc)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Some patients' BP may be elevated in certain medical situations (white coat, pain, anxiety, acute illness)
- Allow the patient to rest, seated or semi-recumbent for example, for 5 minutes before taking their BP where feasible
- Consider taking a manual BP first as a baseline
- Ensure the correct size BP cuff is being used
- There is no place for a systolic-only BP measurement in this context
- Patients with severely elevated BP associated with life-threatening complications such as myocardial ischaemia, pulmonary oedema, stroke, encephalopathy, or aortic syndrome must be treated urgently
- Severely elevated BP without end-organ injury does not usually require rapid prehospital BP lowering
- Hypertensive emergency is defined by acute target-organ injury, not by BP number alone
- Rapid reduction of a severely elevated BP in the absence of hypertensive emergency may cause more harm than good

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Perform repeated vital signs and repeat BP measurements several minutes apart where feasible
- Apply ECG monitoring where available
- Consider Secondary Survey
- If hypertensive emergency is suspected, treat the underlying syndrome and consult the on-call doctor early
- Do not let non-time-critical BP measurement delay care for stroke, ACS, pulmonary oedema, aortic syndrome, or other emergencies

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG
- Consider antihypertensive medication only when clinically indicated by the cause and in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider analgesia if hypertension is secondary to pain or distress
- Avoid reflexive prehospital BP lowering in asymptomatic severe hypertension
- Focus on identifying and treating end-organ complications rather than normalising the BP number

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Hypertensive emergency is defined by acute target-organ injury, not by BP number alone
- Examples of target-organ injury include stroke, encephalopathy, ACS, acute heart failure, aortic syndrome, and eclampsia
- Persistently elevated blood pressure without end-organ injury usually requires outpatient follow-up rather than rapid prehospital correction
